name: 德米特里·肖斯塔科维奇
brief: 肖斯塔科维奇出生于圣彼得堡，是20世纪最伟大的交响曲作曲家。他在苏联政权下度过了一生，经历了斯大林时期的恐怖——1936年《真理报》发表《混乱代替音乐》社论，公开攻击他的歌剧《姆岑斯克县的麦克白夫人》，他差点沦为大清洗的牺牲品。此后他一直在官方要求的「社会主义现实主义」与个人的艺术良知之间痛苦挣扎。他的音乐被认为充满了暗语和双重含义——表面的胜利凯歌下隐藏着讽刺与绝望。
briefEn: Shostakovich was born in St. Petersburg and is widely considered the greatest symphonist of the twentieth century. He spent his entire life under Soviet rule and endured the terrors of the Stalin era — in 1936 Pravda published the editorial "Muddle Instead of Music," a public assault on his opera Lady Macbeth of the Mtsensk District that nearly made him a victim of the Great Purge. From that point on he was caught in an agonizing struggle between the official demand for "Socialist Realism" and his own artistic conscience. His music is thought to be saturated with coded messages and double meanings — beneath the surface of triumphant fanfares lurk irony and despair.
contribution: 肖斯塔科维奇以15部交响曲和15首弦乐四重奏构建了20世纪最宏大的纯器乐叙事体系。他证明了在现代主义浪潮中，传统的调性语言和交响曲形式仍然具有强大的表现力。他的第七交响曲（「列宁格勒」）在二战围城期间的演出成为人类精神不屈的象征。他将政治压迫下的个人悲剧升华为具有普遍意义的音乐史诗。
contributionEn: With fifteen symphonies and fifteen string quartets, Shostakovich built the most monumental body of purely instrumental narrative in the twentieth century. He proved that amid the tides of modernism, the traditional tonal language and the symphonic form still possessed immense expressive power. The performance of his Seventh Symphony ("Leningrad") during the wartime siege became a symbol of the indomitable human spirit. He transmuted the personal tragedy of political oppression into musical epics of universal significance.
style: 肖斯塔科维奇的音乐充满了矛盾和张力——宏大的高潮与空虚的独白、粗暴的讽刺与深切的哀恸、官方的乐观与私人的绝望并存于同一部作品中。他的旋律常常带有一种苦涩的抒情性，和声在调性的框架内大量使用尖锐的不协和音。他善于将日常的音乐素材（进行曲、圆舞曲、加洛普舞曲）扭曲变形，使其获得讽刺或恐怖的效果。
styleEn: Shostakovich's music is shot through with contradiction and tension — grandiose climaxes and hollow soliloquies, savage satire and profound grief, official optimism and private despair coexist within a single work. His melodies often carry a bitter lyricism, while his harmony bristles with sharp dissonance within a tonal framework. He excelled at distorting everyday musical material — marches, waltzes, galops — twisting the familiar into something sardonic or terrifying.

famousScores.name: 《第五交响曲》d小调 Op.47
famousScores.nameEn: Symphony No. 5 in D minor, Op. 47
famousScores.desc: 「一个苏联艺术家对正当批评的创造性回答」——但真的是吗？
famousScores.descEn: "A Soviet artist's creative response to just criticism" — but is it really?
famousScores.detail:
《第五交响曲》（1937）是肖斯塔科维奇在1936年政治风暴后「回归正轨」的宣言作品，也是音乐史上最具争议性的一部交响曲。1936年《真理报》发表社论猛烈批判他的歌剧《麦克白夫人》，斯大林亲自观看后离场表达不满，肖斯塔科维奇随时可能成为大清洗的牺牲品。他撤回了即将首演的第四交响曲，以一部表面上更为传统、更为「积极向上」的第五交响曲作为公开的自我检讨。

第四乐章宏大的终曲（「必须的欢腾」）在当时被苏联官员解读为「对批评的正确回应」，而一位记者甚至记录了首演现场许多观众泪流满面——有人解读为被其音乐的胜利感动，另一些人则认为是被那种强迫欢呼的绝望所压迫而流泪。这到底是真诚的乐观，还是被迫的假笑？这个问题在音乐界争论了半个多世纪。

1979年，苏联流亡者所罗门·沃尔科夫出版了声称是肖斯塔科维奇「回忆录」的《见证》（Testimony），书中肖斯塔科维奇据称说终曲是「枪口下强迫的欢腾」。这本书的真实性至今仍有争议——但不论真相如何，《第五交响曲》的终曲已成为「政治高压下艺术真实性」这一永恒议题的最有力的音乐符号。
famousScores.detailEn:
Symphony No. 5 (1937) was Shostakovich's public declaration of "return to the correct path" after the political storm of 1936, and remains the most contested symphony in music history. After Pravda's devastating editorial and Stalin's own public display of displeasure at his opera Lady Macbeth, Shostakovich was at serious risk of arrest. He withdrew his Fourth Symphony (already rehearsed for premiere) and offered this apparently more traditional, more "uplifting" Fifth as a public act of self-criticism.

The massive finale — the "obligatory rejoicing" — was interpreted by Soviet officials as the correct response to criticism. A journalist recorded that many in the premiere audience wept, which was read by some as being moved by the music's triumphant spirit and by others as being crushed by the despair of forced celebration. Is this genuine optimism or compelled false cheer? This question has been debated for more than half a century.

In 1979, Soviet émigré Solomon Volkov published Testimony, claiming to be Shostakovich's memoirs; in it, Shostakovich allegedly calls the finale "forced rejoicing under the barrel of a gun." The book's authenticity remains disputed — but whatever the truth, the finale of the Fifth Symphony has become the most powerful musical symbol in all of art of the question of artistic authenticity under political coercion.

famousScores.name: 《第七交响曲「列宁格勒」》 Op.60
famousScores.desc: 围城期间的精神武器，二战最著名的交响曲
famousScores.descEn: A spiritual weapon forged during the siege — the most famous symphony of World War II
famousScores.detail:
《第七交响曲「列宁格勒」》（1941）是肖斯塔科维奇在列宁格勒围城（1941—1944年）期间部分写成的，被苏联宣传机器以无与伦比的力度推向了全世界。微缩胶卷乐谱被秘密运送出被围困的城市，先到达德黑兰，再到伦敦，最终到达美国。1942年7月19日，托斯卡尼尼在NBC广播了美国首演，向全美听众直播。

1942年8月9日在列宁格勒围城中的演出是音乐史上最传奇的时刻之一。当时城中能演奏的乐手只剩十五人，其余从前线临时调来，半饿着肚子的乐手们在炮声中演奏。为了压制德军可能的炮击，苏联炮兵专门向德军阵地发动了一次炮击。广播喇叭将音乐播送到被围困的城市，也播送到德军阵地。当晚的指挥家卡尔·叶利亚斯伯格（Karl Eliasberg）后来说，他几乎不确定乐手们是否有力气完成演出。

这部交响曲的历史意义毫无疑问，但其音乐价值争议颇多。第一乐章的「入侵主题」——一段简单旋律以12次渐强重复出现——被一些批评家（包括西方的肖斯塔科维奇崇拜者巴托克）讥讽为过于简单粗暴的音乐宣传。肖斯塔科维奇后来暗示，这个「入侵主题」针对的不仅是德国纳粹，也是斯大林主义——那才是真正入侵了他的生活的力量。
famousScores.detailEn:
Symphony No. 7 "Leningrad" (1941) was partly composed during the Siege of Leningrad (1941–44) and was deployed by the Soviet propaganda machine with unparalleled force. A microfilm of the score was smuggled out of the besieged city, reaching Tehran, then London, then the United States. Toscanini conducted the American premiere on NBC Radio on July 19, 1942, broadcast live to the entire nation.

The performance on August 9, 1942, inside the besieged city of Leningrad, is one of the most legendary moments in music history. Only fifteen musicians were initially available; the rest were recalled from the front, half-starved players performing amid artillery fire. Soviet batteries shelled German positions to suppress the risk of bombardment during the concert. Loudspeakers broadcast the music through the besieged city and across into German lines. Conductor Karl Eliasberg later said he was far from certain the musicians had the physical strength to reach the end.

The symphony's historical significance is unquestioned, but its musical value has always been contested. The first movement's "invasion theme" — a simple tune built through twelve crescendo repetitions — was mocked by some critics (including Bartók, who admired Shostakovich) as crude propaganda. Shostakovich later implied that the "invasion theme" was aimed not only at the German Nazis but at Stalinism — the force that had truly invaded his life.

famousScores.name: 《第八弦乐四重奏》c小调 Op.110
famousScores.nameEn: String Quartet No. 8 in C minor, Op. 110
famousScores.desc: 「献给法西斯主义和战争的牺牲者」——实则是自传性的遗书
famousScores.descEn: "Dedicated to the victims of fascism and war" — in truth, an autobiographical farewell
famousScores.detail:
《第八弦乐四重奏》（1960）是肖斯塔科维奇仅用三天完成的作品，他本人将其视为自己的精神遗嘱。1960年在东德的德累斯顿，目睹了战争废墟（那是二战中被盟军轰炸最惨烈的城市之一）后，肖斯塔科维奇写下了这部作品。他随后写信给友人：「如果我死了，大概没有人会再写一首纪念我的四重奏，所以我为自己写了一首。」

作品以他的音乐签名「DSCH」（D—降E—C—B，德文中他名字首字母的音符化）为核心主题，贯穿整部作品。五个乐章中，他引用了自己的大量作品：第一和第五交响曲、第一钢琴三重奏、以及《麦克白夫人》，还有犹太曲调、革命歌曲，以及一首名为《折磨》的沙皇时代囚歌。这是一部以音乐写成的自传，也是一份对政治恐怖年代的无声控诉。

官方标题「献给法西斯主义和战争的牺牲者」是出于政治需要加上的，但肖斯塔科维奇本人在给好友的私信中说：「这实际上是献给我自己的。」第四乐章尾声处那段孤独的大提琴独奏，是他在整个作品目录中写下的最令人心碎的段落之一。这部四重奏是20世纪室内乐文献中最不可或缺的杰作之一。
famousScores.detailEn:
The String Quartet No. 8 (1960) was composed in just three days and stands as Shostakovich's spiritual testament. In Dresden, East Germany — one of the cities most devastated by Allied bombing in the Second World War — after witnessing the ruins, he composed this work. He wrote soon after to a friend: "If I die, I don't suppose anyone will write a quartet in memory of me, so I wrote one myself."

The work is built on his musical signature "DSCH" (D—E-flat—C—B, the German-letter transliteration of his name as musical notes), which permeates the entire quartet. Across its five movements he quotes extensively from his own works: the First and Fifth Symphonies, the First Piano Trio, Lady Macbeth of the Mtsensk District — alongside a Jewish tune, a revolutionary song, and a tsarist-era prisoner's song called "Tormented by the Chains of Grievous Bondage." It is an autobiography in music, a silent indictment of years of political terror.

The official dedication — "to the victims of fascism and war" — was added for political necessity; in private letters Shostakovich told close friends: "This is really dedicated to myself." The solitary cello solo at the end of the fourth movement is one of the most heartbreaking passages in his entire catalog. The quartet is among the most indispensable masterpieces in the twentieth-century chamber music literature.

famousScores.name: 《第二圆舞曲》
famousScores.desc: 选自《综艺管弦乐组曲》（常被误称为「爵士组曲」），近年来最广为流传的古典旋律之一
famousScores.descEn: From the Suite for Variety Orchestra — one of the most widely recognized classical melodies in recent years
famousScores.detail:
《第二圆舞曲》（约1956年）选自肖斯塔科维奇的《综艺管弦乐组曲》（Suite for Variety Orchestra），这套作品长期以来被误称为「第二爵士组曲」，直到21世纪初才被音乐学家正确归档。这首圆舞曲以其优雅而略带忧郁的旋律，成为肖斯塔科维奇最广为人知的通俗作品，在影视作品中被无数次使用，最著名的是斯坦利·库布里克在《大开眼界》（Eyes Wide Shut，1999）中的运用。

这首圆舞曲体现了肖斯塔科维奇作品的一个独特面向——轻盈、几近通俗的外表，掩藏着一种令人难以言说的忧伤。那段圆舞曲旋律优美流畅，几乎像是一首真实的舞厅音乐，但其和声语言和情感质感，却有着肖斯塔科维奇式的距离感和苦涩，如同在灯火通明的宴会厅内独自悲伤的人。

《第二圆舞曲》在今天的大众文化中获得了极高的知名度，这与它被广泛用于影视配乐密切相关。它成为肖斯塔科维奇音乐「走进寻常百姓家」的最有力媒介，也是许多人初次接触这位作曲家的敲门砖。它的流行程度远超肖斯塔科维奇本人可能预见的范围，在某种意义上成为了当代的「经典流行」（classical pop）。
famousScores.detailEn:
Waltz No. 2 (c. 1956) is from Shostakovich's Suite for Variety Orchestra — a work long misidentified as the "Second Jazz Suite" until correctly catalogued by musicologists in the early twenty-first century. With its graceful and faintly melancholy melody, this waltz has become Shostakovich's best-known popular piece, used innumerable times in film and television — most famously by Stanley Kubrick in Eyes Wide Shut (1999).

The waltz exemplifies a distinctive dimension of Shostakovich's output — a light, almost popular surface concealing an inexpressible sadness. The melody is smooth and beautiful, almost indistinguishable from genuine ballroom music, yet its harmonic language and emotional texture carry that characteristic Shostakovichian distance and bitterness — like someone quietly grieving in a brightly lit banquet hall.

Waltz No. 2 has achieved extraordinary popular recognition through its extensive use in film and television scores. It is the most powerful vehicle by which Shostakovich's music has entered ordinary people's lives, and the first point of contact with this composer for many listeners. Its popularity far exceeds anything Shostakovich himself could have predicted, making it in a sense a piece of contemporary "classical pop."

famousScores.name: 《姆岑斯克县的麦克白夫人》
famousScores.nameEn: Lady Macbeth of the Mtsensk District
famousScores.desc: 引发政治风暴的歌剧，音乐的戏剧力量惊人
famousScores.descEn: The opera that triggered a political storm — staggering in its dramatic musical power
famousScores.detail:
《姆岑斯克县的麦克白夫人》（1930—1932年作，1934年首演）是肖斯塔科维奇唯一的重要歌剧，也是苏联音乐史上最戏剧性的政治事件的中心。歌剧改编自俄国作家列斯科夫的同名小说，讲述一个沙皇时代商人之妻卡捷琳娜·伊兹梅洛娃的故事：她与工人私通、毒杀公公、谋杀丈夫，最终在流放西伯利亚途中投河自尽。

1936年1月，斯大林在克里姆林宫观看了这部歌剧并中途离席，两天后《真理报》发表了著名社论《混乱代替音乐》（Muddle Instead of Music）——措辞之严厉超过了任何此前针对艺术家的官方批评。文章指控这部歌剧充满了「噪音和夸张」、「刺耳的、杂乱的、神经错乱的」音乐，是「形式主义」的体现。肖斯塔科维奇的日子顿时岌岌可危，他感到随时可能被捕，据他后来描述，他每晚都在担心「门铃声」的到来。

作为歌剧本身，《麦克白夫人》是一部令人叹为观止的音乐戏剧：间奏曲的粗犷讽刺、凶案场景的惊心动魄、卡捷琳娜悲歌的深情抒发，共同构成了俄国歌剧文献中20世纪最有力的作品之一。它经过修改后以《卡捷琳娜·伊兹梅洛娃》的名称在斯大林死后复演，直到今天仍是国际歌剧院的重要保留曲目。
famousScores.detailEn:
Lady Macbeth of the Mtsensk District (composed 1930–32; premiered 1934) is Shostakovich's only major opera and the center of the most dramatic political event in Soviet music history. Adapted from Leskov's novella, it tells of Katerina Izmailova, a merchant's wife in Tsarist Russia who takes a lover, poisons her father-in-law, murders her husband, and ultimately drowns herself during deportation to Siberia.

In January 1936, Stalin attended a Kremlin performance and left before the end; two days later Pravda published the notorious editorial "Muddle Instead of Music" — the harshest official assault on any artist in Soviet history. The article accused the opera of "noise and grimaces," "cacophonous, muddled, neuropathic" music, and "formalism." Shostakovich's position became immediately precarious; by his own later account, he feared arrest every night, listening for the "doorbell" that would announce his fate.

As an opera in its own right, Lady Macbeth is a staggeringly powerful music drama: the brutal satire of the intermezzos, the terrifying vividness of the murder scenes, and the deeply felt lyricism of Katerina's grief together constitute the most forceful Russian operatic work of the twentieth century. Revised and retitled Katerina Izmailova after Stalin's death, it remains a major presence in international opera house repertoire to this day.

famousScores.name: 《第十交响曲》e小调 Op.93
famousScores.nameEn: Symphony No. 10 in E minor, Op. 93
famousScores.desc: 斯大林死后的第一部交响曲，最私人的自白与最隐秘的情感表达
famousScores.descEn: The first symphony after Stalin's death — Shostakovich's most personal confession in orchestral form
famousScores.detail:
《第十交响曲》（1953）是肖斯塔科维奇斯大林死后（1953年3月）创作的第一部交响曲，也是他整个交响曲创作中最被音乐学家反复解读的作品之一。斯大林在世的最后两年（1951—1953），肖斯塔科维奇几乎停止了重要作品的创作——斯大林一死，他立即完成了这部酝酿已久的交响曲，似乎压抑多年的音乐洪流终于找到了出口。

第二乐章是整部交响曲中最暴烈的段落——约四分钟的极速狂飙，全弦乐、铜管、打击乐以凶猛的齐奏席卷一切，情绪既像愤怒又像恐惧。许多学者（包括俄国音乐学家博里斯·特什查尔科夫斯基）认为这是对斯大林的直接音乐「肖像」。第三乐章的圆号独奏以「DSCH」主题为核心，是肖斯塔科维奇在作品中「签名」的最明显体现。

1953年12月17日在列宁格勒首演，由姆拉文斯基指挥，引发了关于其含义的热烈讨论。随后在苏联作曲家协会召开的长达三天的讨论会上，各方对于这部作品的「正确性」展开了激烈辩论——这在某种程度上是斯大林时代结束后第一次真正公开的音乐美学讨论。第十交响曲至今被认为是20世纪交响曲文献中最重要的作品之一。
famousScores.detailEn:
Symphony No. 10 (1953) is the first major symphony Shostakovich completed after Stalin's death (March 1953) and is one of the works most persistently analyzed and reinterpreted by musicologists across his entire symphonic output. During Stalin's final two years (1951–53), Shostakovich had nearly ceased producing important compositions — Stalin's death seemed to release a flood of music he had been holding back for years.

The second movement is the most violent passage in the symphony — roughly four minutes of headlong, furious tempo in which full strings, brass, and percussion surge through everything in unison, the emotion simultaneously like rage and like terror. Many scholars, including Russian musicologist Boris Tishchenko, have argued this is a direct musical "portrait" of Stalin. The horn solo of the third movement, built on the DSCH motif, is the most conspicuous example of Shostakovich's musical self-signature.

The Leningrad premiere on December 17, 1953, conducted by Mravinsky, triggered intense debate about its meaning. A three-day conference of the Soviet Composers' Union that followed engaged in fierce public discussion of the work's "correctness" — in some respects the first genuinely open musical-aesthetic debate since the end of the Stalin era. The Tenth remains widely considered one of the most important symphonies in the twentieth-century literature.

famousScores.name: 《第十三交响曲「巴比亚尔」》 Op.113
famousScores.desc: 以叶夫图申科的诗歌为文本，对反犹主义的公开控诉，苏联当局极度不安的作品
famousScores.descEn: Setting Yevtushenko's poetry — a public indictment of anti-Semitism that deeply alarmed Soviet authorities
famousScores.detail:
《第十三交响曲「巴比亚尔」》（1962）是肖斯塔科维奇最具政治勇气的作品，以诗人叶夫根尼·叶夫图申科（Yevgeny Yevtushenko）的五首诗歌为文本，由男低音独唱和男低音合唱演唱，管弦乐伴奏。第一首诗歌《巴比亚尔》直接描写了1941年纳粹在乌克兰基辅郊外的巴比亚尔山谷杀死33,771名犹太人的历史事件，并严厉批判苏联官方对这一反犹太暴行的沉默和漠视。

这部交响曲的首演（1962年12月18日，指挥基里尔·孔德拉辛）本身就是一场政治事件。苏联当局千方百计阻挠演出——两位原定独唱者被当局施压后临时退出，由另外两位年轻歌手顶上。首演当晚，莫斯科音乐界的精英们挤满了音乐厅，在党的干部缺席的情况下，观众给予了长达15分钟的起立鼓掌。

苏联当局随后对这部作品施加了巨大压力——叶夫图申科被迫修改了部分诗词，试图淡化其明确的批判含义。但修改后的版本从未被肖斯塔科维奇接受为「正确」版本。今天，《第十三交响曲》是肖斯塔科维奇交响曲中表达政治立场最明确的一首，也是20世纪声乐交响曲的杰作，以其无畏的道德勇气和深刻的人道主义精神，在音乐史上占据了不可磨灭的地位。
famousScores.detailEn:
Symphony No. 13 "Babi Yar" (1962) is the most politically courageous of Shostakovich's works, setting five poems by Yevgeny Yevtushenko for bass soloist, bass chorus, and orchestra. The opening poem, "Babi Yar," directly addresses the 1941 Nazi massacre of 33,771 Jews in a ravine outside Kyiv, Ukraine — and harshly indicts Soviet official silence and indifference toward this act of antisemitic slaughter.

The premiere (December 18, 1962, conducted by Kirill Kondrashin) was itself a political event. Soviet authorities made every effort to impede the performance — two of the originally scheduled soloists withdrew under official pressure and were replaced by two young singers at short notice. On the evening of the premiere, the Moscow musical elite packed the hall; in the absence of Communist Party functionaries, the audience gave a fifteen-minute standing ovation.

Soviet authorities subsequently applied enormous pressure — Yevtushenko was compelled to revise sections of the poetry to dilute its explicit criticism. But Shostakovich never accepted the revised version as "correct." Today the Thirteenth Symphony is the most politically explicit of Shostakovich's symphonies and a masterpiece of the twentieth-century vocal-symphony genre, holding an indelible place in music history for its fearless moral courage and profound humanist spirit.

famousScores.name: 《g小调钢琴五重奏》 Op.57
famousScores.nameEn: Piano Quintet in G minor, Op. 57
famousScores.desc: 斯大林文化奖获奖作品，室内乐中最完美的「肖斯塔科维奇式对话」
famousScores.descEn: Stalin Prize winner — the most perfect embodiment of Shostakovichian dialogue in chamber music
famousScores.detail:
《g小调钢琴五重奏》（1940）是肖斯塔科维奇室内乐创作的顶峰之一，也是他获得斯大林文化奖的作品（1941）——这是一个极为奇特的事实，因为这部作品无论从风格还是情感质感来看，都丝毫不像「社会主义现实主义」的典范，而更像是巴赫的对位精神与肖斯塔科维奇个人悲情的完美融合。

五个乐章中，前奏曲与赋格（前两个乐章）展现了巴赫式的对位写作，钢琴与弦乐四重奏以严密的赋格手法展开，但其中的和声语言是彻底肖斯塔科维奇式的——那种特有的半音游走和尖锐不协和音，使巴赫的骨架拥有了截然不同的情感肌肤。诙谐曲乐章以暴烈的节奏冲击和讽刺性的军乐旋律，展现了肖斯塔科维奇最独特的风格侧面。

这部五重奏由肖斯塔科维奇本人担任钢琴，与贝多芬四重奏团合作首演，演出极为成功。它是他室内乐作品中最易为一般听众接受的一首，在技法深度与情感可及性之间取得了难得的平衡，证明了肖斯塔科维奇在弦乐四重奏以外同样拥有无与伦比的室内乐写作才华。
famousScores.detailEn:
The Piano Quintet in G minor (1940) is one of the summits of Shostakovich's chamber music output, and the work for which he received the Stalin Prize (1941) — a peculiarly ironic fact, since the piece bears no resemblance to any "Socialist Realist" model either in style or emotional texture; it is instead a perfect fusion of Bachian counterpoint with Shostakovich's personal grief.

The first two movements — Prelude and Fugue — display Bachian contrapuntal writing as piano and string quartet engage in rigorous fugal dialogue; but the harmonic language is entirely Shostakovichian — that characteristic chromatic drift and biting dissonance give a Bachian skeleton entirely different emotional flesh. The scherzo movement, with its violent rhythmic assault and satirical march melody, shows one of Shostakovich's most distinctive stylistic faces.

The premiere, with Shostakovich himself at the piano alongside the Beethoven Quartet, was an outstanding success. This is the most accessible of his chamber works to general audiences, achieving a rare balance between technical depth and emotional accessibility, demonstrating that Shostakovich possessed chamber music mastery of equal quality to his string quartets.

famousScores.name: 《e小调钢琴三重奏第二首》 Op.67
famousScores.nameEn: Piano Trio No. 2 in E minor, Op. 67
famousScores.desc: 献给亡友索列尔金，以犹太音乐风格写成的安魂曲
famousScores.descEn: A memorial to his fallen friend Sollertinsky — a requiem permeated by Jewish musical idiom
famousScores.detail:
《e小调钢琴三重奏第二首》（1944）是肖斯塔科维奇最深情的室内乐作品，献给他最亲密的朋友、音乐学家伊万·索列尔金（Ivan Sollertinsky）——在作品完成的同年，索列尔金因心脏病突然去世，享年41岁。肖斯塔科维奇与索列尔金的友谊长达25年，是他动荡生命中最稳固的精神支柱之一。

作品共四个乐章，末乐章最为震撼——肖斯塔科维奇在这里引入了一段犹太主题，用一个近似犹太舞蹈音乐（klezmer）风格的旋律，让人们在「死亡华尔兹」的节奏下起舞。这一意象来自战争期间流传的一个残酷传说：纳粹在屠杀犹太人之前，曾强迫他们在自己挖好的坑边跳舞。这段舞蹈旋律在三重奏中以近乎残酷的轻快演奏，其中的反讽之痛，在整个音乐史上都是最令人心碎的时刻之一。

犹太音乐元素在肖斯塔科维奇晚期作品中占有重要地位——他将犹太音乐视为一种在苦难中保持尊严的象征，也是一种能够「在灾难中微笑」（他的原话）的特殊能力。这部三重奏是他最直接运用这一元素的作品，也是20世纪三重奏文献中最具情感深度的杰作之一。
famousScores.detailEn:
The Piano Trio No. 2 in E minor (1944) is Shostakovich's most deeply felt chamber work, dedicated to his closest friend, the musicologist Ivan Sollertinsky, who died suddenly of heart failure in the same year the work was completed, at the age of forty-one. Shostakovich and Sollertinsky's friendship had lasted twenty-five years — one of the most stable spiritual anchors of Shostakovich's turbulent life.

The four-movement work reaches its most shattering moment in the finale, where Shostakovich introduces a Jewish theme — a melody in the style of klezmer dance music — set against the rhythm of a "death waltz." This image draws on a wartime story that circulated widely: that the Nazis had forced Jews to dance at the edge of the mass graves they had dug before shooting them. The dance melody is played with almost cruel lightness in the trio, and the irony of pain embedded in it is one of the most heartbreaking passages in all of music history.

Jewish musical elements play an important role in Shostakovich's later work — he regarded Jewish music as a symbol of dignity maintained under suffering, a special ability to "smile in the face of catastrophe" (his own words). This trio is his most direct deployment of this element, and one of the works of the deepest emotional weight in the entire twentieth-century trio literature.

famousScores.name: 《第一大提琴协奏曲》 Op.107
famousScores.desc: 为大提琴家罗斯特罗波维奇而作，极具原创性的大提琴协奏曲
famousScores.descEn: Written for Rostropovich — an intensely original contribution to the cello concerto repertoire
famousScores.detail:
《第一大提琴协奏曲》（1959）是肖斯塔科维奇为他的密友、苏联大提琴宗师姆斯季斯拉夫·罗斯特罗波维奇（Mstislav Rostropovich）而作，也是两人深厚友谊的音乐结晶。传说肖斯塔科维奇完成这部作品后，罗斯特罗波维奇在三天内就将其记熟并演奏给他听——作曲家当场目瞪口呆。作品1959年10月4日在列宁格勒首演，指挥叶夫根尼·姆拉文斯基，罗斯特罗波维奇担任独奏。

这部协奏曲共四个乐章，以「DSCH」主题的变形为贯穿全曲的核心材料。第一乐章以简练、稍带讽刺口吻的主题开篇，一反传统协奏曲的宏大入场；慢乐章（第二乐章）是全曲情感的中心，大提琴在木管乐的陪伴下奏出一段苦涩而深情的「夜曲」，是肖斯塔科维奇最动人的慢板之一；第三乐章是大提琴的无伴奏华彩，长达数分钟；第四乐章以大提琴与管弦乐队的「争斗」收束全曲。

《第一大提琴协奏曲》彻底改变了大提琴协奏曲的版图——它创造了一种全新的独奏大提琴与管弦乐的对话方式：大提琴不是被管弦乐「衬托」的主角，而是与管弦乐处于近乎平等、甚至对抗的关系之中。这部作品至今仍是大提琴演奏家必须掌握的核心曲目，也是20世纪协奏曲文献中最原创、最具深度的作品之一。
famousScores.detailEn:
The Cello Concerto No. 1 (1959) was composed for Shostakovich's close friend and the supreme Soviet cellist Mstislav Rostropovich, a musical crystallization of their deep friendship. Legend has it that after Shostakovich finished the score, Rostropovich memorized and performed it back for him in three days — leaving the composer speechless. The premiere on October 4, 1959 in Leningrad was conducted by Yevgeny Mravinsky, with Rostropovich as soloist.

The four-movement concerto uses transformations of the DSCH motto as its permeating core material. The first movement opens with a terse, faintly ironic theme, rejecting the grand entrance of the traditional concerto; the slow second movement is the emotional center, with the cello tracing a bittersweet nocturne above woodwind accompaniment — one of Shostakovich's most moving slow passages; the third movement is an extended unaccompanied cadenza for the cello; the finale pits cello against orchestra in fierce struggle.

The Cello Concerto No. 1 fundamentally transformed the landscape of the cello concerto — it created a wholly new dialogue between solo cello and orchestra, one in which the cello is not "supported" by the orchestra but stands in near-equal, even adversarial relationship with it. The work remains a cornerstone of the cellist's essential repertoire and one of the most original and deeply considered concertos of the twentieth century.

influence: 肖斯塔科维奇证明了即使在极端政治压迫下，艺术良知仍可以通过隐喻和双重编码存活下来。他的交响曲传统由阿尔弗雷德·施尼特克和索菲亚·古拜杜丽娜继承。他的弦乐四重奏影响了英国作曲家布里顿（两人是亲密的朋友）。他关于「艺术家与国家权力」关系的命运至今仍是知识分子讨论的核心话题。
influenceEn: Shostakovich demonstrated that even under extreme political oppression, artistic conscience can survive through metaphor and double coding. His symphonic tradition was carried forward by Alfred Schnittke and Sofia Gubaidulina. His string quartets profoundly influenced the English composer Britten, who was a close personal friend. The question his fate raises — the relationship between the artist and state power — remains a central topic of intellectual debate to this day.
